en.extract:
The Javan plover (Anarhynchus javanicus) is a Charadriidae species with an unclear taxonomic status. Endemic to Indonesia and Timor-Leste, it inhabits sandy shores and intertidal mudflats, where it is thought to breed year-round. Its diet mainly consists of crustaceans, worms, and mollusks. It is mostly threatened by human disturbance and coastal development in Southeast Asia, and is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.


Description

The Javan plover is a small 15 cm (5.9 in) shorebird with a reddish-brown coloration that begins at the crown and extends from the back of the neck onto the sides of the breast, with a smaller dark patch below. The breast collar is also reddish-brown, while the hind neck collar is white and always incomplete. The upperparts are brown or grey brown, and its underparts are white. Its head plumage is characterized by a black bar on the forecrown and a dark brown horizontal eyestripe. Its supercilium is typically white and extends behind the eye. The head is generally oval-shaped. The black bill is about 12–16 mm (0.47–0.63 in) and is blunt and thick at the base. White outer tail feathers and a distinct white bar across the upper wing can be seen during flight. In breeding plumage, the Javan plover has clear and usually complete yellowish-brown lateral breast patches.
Breeding males have a short white supercilium, a bright white forehead and darker coloration. Compared to males, breeding females have a lighter, browner cap, full dark patches on the sides of the chest, and yellowish-tan supercilia that stretch past the eyes. Some females may also have a darker brown coloration or a combination of gray-brown and yellow-brown undertones. Unlike males, females do not have rich reddish-brown tones in their plumage, and non-breeding males look similar as breeding females, as both have a partial collar and lighter, less defined patches on the sides of their breast.
While the Javan plover resembles the Kentish plover (Anarhynchus alexandrinus), the Malaysian plover (Anarhynchus peronii), and the red-capped plover (Anarhynchus ruficapillus), it can be distinguished by its paler legs, longer tibia, single patches on the sides of its chest, and its incomplete white collar on the back of its neck. The juvenile can be told apart by its paler and more diffused breast-side patches and its light brownish plain upperparts with a slightly paler forehead and supercilium. Its wing coverts are small and neatly arranged with yellowish-brown fringes. Following post-juvenile molt, the Javan plover keeps its wing coverts, although the majority of the upperparts are replaced.


Taxonomy
The Javan plover was first described in 1938 by Frederick Nutter Chasen who proposed the scientific name Charadrius alexandrinus javanicus classifying it as a subspecies of the Kentish plover. The holotype specimen was captured in March 1936 along the coast near Jakarta on the island of Java, and additional paratypes comprising three males and five females were also collected on Java. It is a member of the order Charadriiformes and the family Charadriidae.
The Javan plover is currently treated as a separate species and is monotypic, although its taxonomic status remains uncertain. Following phylogenetic analyses, it was moved from the genus Charadrius to the genus Anarhynchus, along with most species formerly placed in Charadrius.
The Javan plover is closely related to the white-fronted plover (Anarhynchus marginatus), the Kentish plover, the white-faced plover (Anarhynchus dealbatus), the snowy plover (Anarhynchus nivosus), and the red-capped plover, all of which have been occasionally classified as a single species. The Javan plover has been considered most often a subspecies of the Kentish plover because of low genetic divergence (1.2%). It has also been associated with the red-capped plover, or, in some cases, the Malaysian plover, despite apparent morphological differences.


Distribution and habitat

The Javan plover was previously considered to be endemic to the Indonesian islands of Java, Bali and the Kangean Islands. However, it was also sighted in southern Sumatra, Madura, Lombok, southwestern Sulawesi, Sumbawa, Flores, western and central Lesser Sundas, Meno Island, Semau and Timor-Leste.
The Javan plover is sedentary. It inhabits sandy beaches, coastal plains, inland fishponds and rice fields. Open flats with minimal vegetation and a high cover of bare rock are typically favored. It has also been observed up to 30 km (19 mi) inland on Java.


Behavior


Vocalizations
Because the Javan plover is classified as a subspecies of the Kentish plover in most scientific literature, vocalization data are mostly limited to reports of a repeated single soft, rising “kweek”.
While the Kentish plover produces a shorter “krk-krk…pik” or “pik” call, the Javan plover gives a longer “kweek……kweek” or a single drawn-out “kweek”.
When adults attempt to protect their chicks or nests from humans, they call repeatedly with a soft, rising “tu-wit” while performing apparent distraction behaviors to divert attention, where they either mimic the quick, erratic movements of a fleeing rodent (i.e., “rodent-run display”) or feign injury by dragging a wing (i.e., “broken-wing display”)


Diet
The Javan plover has a similar diet to the Kentish plover, although not much data is available. Near the shore, it feeds on crustaceans, polychaete worms, and mollusks. Like other small plovers, the Javan plover has been recorded foraging for crabs at low tide by lowering its head and quickly running across the sand to capture prey. When inland, it can also prey on insects like beetles and flies, and spiders. The Javan plover has been observed foraging in dry, cleared rice-fields before seeding or after harvest.


Reproduction
Very little information is available regarding the breeding behavior of the Javan plover. Breeding is suspected to occur year-round, during both wet and dry seasons. The peak breeding season is believed to take place from May to August. A second breeding season may also occur between September and January to February. In West Java, chicks have been observed as early as late April. In eastern Java, egg-laying occurs in June, September, and January, and hatching has been recorded in June and August, while courtship or territorial displays have been observed from May to August. Observations of breeding activity have been reported on Sulawesi in early October, in southeastern Sumatra and Timor-Leste in July, and on Bali in June.

The Javan plover breeds in coastal and inland habitats far from human activity (2-5 km away), including open dune slacks, shores of rivers and lakes, marshes, dry plains, rice fields, and dry aquaculture ponds. The nest usually consists of a small cavity about 4 cm deep and is lined with shells of mud snails (Pirenella cingulata).
The nesting site is prepared collaboratively by both sexes. Ground-nesting shorebirds, including plovers, select their nesting site based on specific habitat characteristics, such as substrate particle size and vegetation cover.
During the breeding season, some Javan plovers were observed attacking larger predatory birds to protect their chicks. Documented predators of both chicks and eggs include the long-tailed shrike (Lanius schach), the Sunda crow (Corvus enca), and the Javan mongoose (Herpestes javanicus).

Eggs of the Javan plover are light beige to dark olive-cream, and the shell has many black markings, typically interspersed with grayish or sepia undertones. A typical clutch consists of four eggs, each measuring approximately 23 mm (0.91 in) in width and 40 mm in length. Both parents contribute to incubation They often dampen their underparts with water before taking turns sitting on the eggs, likely to keep the eggs from overheating during high temperatures, and the incubation lasts between 21 and 23 days.
Hatchlings closely resemble those of other small Charadriidae plovers.
Chicks are precocious and leave the nest shortly after birth. After hatching, they can immediately walk and forage for food independently, although they remain under parental care for up to three months.


Threats and conservation status
The Javan plover is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List. The global population is estimated between 2,000 and 6,000 individuals, Population numbers are thought to be stable. Current threats include egg-collecting, human disturbance, and coastal development under increasing tourism pressures in Southeast Asia.


References


External links
Javan plover on eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Javan plover on Avibase
Javan plover sound recordings on Xeno-Canto
Javan plover pictures on the Macaulay Library (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Interactive range map of Charadrius javanicus on the IUCN Red List
Javan plover foraging video on YouTube
Javan plover nesting video on YouTube

The '''Javan plover''' ('''''Anarhynchus javanicus''''') is a [[Charadriidae]] species with an unclear taxonomic status. [[Endemism|Endemic]] to Indonesia and Timor-Leste, it inhabits sandy shores and intertidal [[mudflat]]s, where it is thought to breed year-round. Its diet mainly consists of crustaceans, worms, and mollusks. It is mostly threatened by human disturbance and coastal development in Southeast Asia, and is listed as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].

== Description ==
[[File:Javan Plover - Jakarta Bay - West Java, Indonesia.jpg|thumb|Javan plover in Jakarta bay area]]
The Javan plover is a small {{cvt|15|cm}} shorebird with a reddish-brown coloration that begins at the crown and extends from the back of the neck onto the sides of the breast, with a smaller dark patch below.<ref name=Wiersma2023>{{Cite taxon|BOW|last1=Wiersma |first1=P. |last2=Kirwan |first2=G. M. |date=2023 |title=Javan Plover (''Anarhynchus javanicus'')|version=1.1|url-access=subscription |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/javplo1/cur/introduction |doi=10.2173/bow.javplo1.01.1}}</ref> The breast collar is also reddish-brown, while the hind neck collar is white and always incomplete.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Iqbal |first1=M. |last2=Taufiqurrahman |first2=I. |last3=Gilfedder |first3=M. |last4=Baskoro |first4=K. |date=2013 |title=Field identification of Javan Plover ''Charadrius javanicus'' |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/262562330 |journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin |volume=120 |issue=2 |pages=96–101}}</ref> The upperparts are brown or grey brown, and its underparts are white.<ref name=":6" /> Its head plumage is characterized by a black bar on the forecrown and a dark brown horizontal eyestripe.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> Its [[supercilium]] is typically white and extends behind the eye.<ref name=":6" /> The head is generally oval-shaped.<ref name=":6" /> The black bill is about {{cvt|12-16|mm}} and is blunt and thick at the base.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> White outer tail feathers and a distinct white bar across the upper wing can be seen during flight.<ref name=":6" /> In breeding plumage, the Javan plover has clear and usually complete yellowish-brown lateral breast patches.<ref name=":6" />

Breeding males have a short white supercilium, a bright white forehead and darker coloration.<ref name=":6" /> Compared to males, breeding females have a lighter, browner cap, full dark patches on the sides of the chest, and yellowish-tan supercilia that stretch past the eyes.<ref name=Febrianto2016>{{Cite journal |last1=Febrianto |first1=I. |last2=Handono, C.D. |last3=Tirtaningtyas |first3=F. N. |date=2016 |title=Behavior of breeding Javan plover ''Charadrius javanicus'' at Wonorejo fishponds, Surabaya, Indonesia |journal=Stilt |pages=77–78 |volume=69–70 |url=https://awsg.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/Stilt-69-70.pdf#page=79}}</ref> Some females may also have a darker brown coloration or a combination of gray-brown and yellow-brown undertones.<ref name=":6" /> Unlike males, females do not have rich reddish-brown tones in their plumage, and non-breeding males look similar as breeding females, as both have a partial collar and lighter, less defined patches on the sides of their breast.<ref name=Wiersma2023/>

While the Javan plover resembles the [[Kentish plover]] (''Anarhynchus alexandrinus''), the [[Malaysian plover]] (''Anarhynchus peronii''), and the [[red-capped plover]] (''Anarhynchus ruficapillus''), it can be distinguished by its paler legs, longer tibia, single patches on the sides of its chest, and its incomplete white collar on the back of its neck.<ref name=":6" /> The juvenile can be told apart by its paler and more diffused breast-side patches and its light brownish plain upperparts with a slightly paler forehead and supercilium.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> Its [[Covert feather|wing coverts]] are small and neatly arranged with yellowish-brown fringes.<ref name=":6" /> Following post-juvenile molt, the Javan plover keeps its wing coverts, although the majority of the upperparts are replaced.<ref name=Wiersma2023/>

== Taxonomy ==
The Javan plover was first described in 1938 by [[Frederick Nutter Chasen]] who proposed the scientific name ''Charadrius alexandrinus javanicus'' classifying it as a subspecies of the [[Kentish plover]].<ref name=Chasen>{{Cite journal |last=Chasen |first=F. N. |date=1938 |title=Vier neue Vogelrassen aus Malaysia |url=https://www.zobodat.at/pdf/Ornithologische-Monatberichte_46_0005-0008.pdf |journal=Ornithologische Monatsberichte |pages=5–8}}</ref> The [[holotype]] specimen was captured in March 1936 along the coast near Jakarta on the island of Java, and additional [[paratype]]s comprising three males and five females were also collected on Java.<ref name=Chasen/> It is a member of the order [[Charadriiformes]] and the family [[Charadriidae]].<ref name=BirdLife2021>{{Cite web |last=BirdLife International |date=2021 |title=Species factsheet: Javan Plover ''Charadrius javanicus'' |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/javan-plover-charadrius-javanicus |access-date=2025-10-13 |website=BirdLife DataZone}}</ref>

The Javan plover is currently treated as a separate species and is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]], although its taxonomic status remains uncertain.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> Following phylogenetic analyses, it was moved from the genus ''[[Charadrius]]'' to the genus ''[[Anarhynchus]]'', along with most species formerly placed in ''Charadrius''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Černý |first1=D. |last2=Natale |first2=R. |date=2022 |title=Comprehensive taxon sampling and vetted fossils help clarify the time tree of shorebirds (Aves, Charadriiformes) |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=177 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2022.107620 |article-number=107620 |pmid=36038056 |bibcode=2022MolPE.17707620C}}</ref>

The Javan plover is closely related to the [[white-fronted plover]] (''Anarhynchus marginatus''), the Kentish plover, the [[white-faced plover]] (''Anarhynchus dealbatus''), the [[snowy plover]] (''Anarhynchus'' ''nivosus''), and the [[red-capped plover]], all of which have been occasionally classified as a single species.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> The Javan plover has been considered most often a subspecies of the Kentish plover because of low genetic divergence (1.2%).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ashari |first1=H. |last2=Astuti |first2=D. |date=2017 |title=Study on Phylogenetic Status of Javan Plover Bird (Charadrius, Charadriidae, Charadriiformes) through DNA Barcoding Analysis |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/368143328 |journal=Biosaintifika Journal of Biology & Biology Education |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=49–57 |doi=10.15294/biosaintifika.v9i1.9195|doi-access=free }}</ref> It has also been associated with the red-capped plover, or, in some cases, the [[Malaysian plover]], despite apparent morphological differences.<ref name=Febrianto2016/>

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Charadrius javanicus, South Lumajang, East Java, Indonesia 181228-P900330 jtg.jpg|thumb|Javan plover on a sandy beach in South Lumajang, East Java, Indonesia]]
The Javan plover was previously considered to be [[Endemism|endemic]] to the Indonesian islands of Java, Bali and the Kangean Islands.<ref name=BirdLife2021/> However, it was also sighted in southern Sumatra, [[Madura]], [[Lombok]], southwestern Sulawesi, [[Sumbawa]], [[Flores]], western and central Lesser Sundas, Meno Island, [[Semau]] and Timor-Leste.<ref name=BirdLife2021/><ref name=":6" />

The Javan plover is sedentary.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> It inhabits sandy beaches, [[coastal plain]]s, inland fishponds and rice fields.<ref name=BirdLife2021/> Open flats with minimal vegetation and a high cover of bare rock are typically favored.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> It has also been observed up to {{cvt|30|km}} inland on Java.<ref name=":6" />

== Behavior ==
=== Vocalizations ===
{{Birdsong|species="Javan plover"|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Charadrius-javanicus|image=Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg}}Because the Javan plover is classified as a subspecies of the [[Kentish plover]] in most scientific literature, vocalization data are mostly limited to reports of a repeated single soft, rising “''kweek''”.<ref name=Wiersma2023/>
While the Kentish plover produces a shorter “''krk-krk…pik''” or “''pik''” call, the Javan plover gives a longer “''kweek……kweek''” or a single drawn-out “''kweek''”.<ref name=":6" />

When adults attempt to protect their chicks or nests from humans, they call repeatedly with a soft, rising “''tu-wit''” while performing apparent [[Distraction display|distraction behaviors]] to divert attention, where they either mimic the quick, erratic movements of a fleeing rodent (i.e., “rodent-run display”) or feign injury by dragging a wing (i.e., “broken-wing display”)<ref name=Taufiqurrahman2013>{{Cite journal |last1=Taufiqurrahman |first1=I. |last2=Subekti |first2=H. |date=2013 |title=Distraction behaviour of breeding Javan Plover ''Charadrius javanicus'' |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259174049 |journal=Kukila |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=17–21}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
The Javan plover has a similar diet to the [[Kentish plover]], although not much data is available.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> Near the shore, it feeds on [[crustacean]]s, [[Polychaete|polychaete worms]], and [[Mollusca|mollusks]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J. |last2=Wiersma |first2=P. |last3=Kirwan |first3=G. M. |last4=Collar |first4=N. |last5=Boesman |first5=P. F. D. |last6=Sharpe |first6=C. J. |date=2023 |title=Kentish Plover (''Anarhynchus alexandrinus''), version 1.2 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/kenplo1/cur/introduction |work=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.kenplo1.01.2}}</ref> Like other small plovers, the Javan plover has been recorded foraging for crabs at low tide by lowering its head and quickly running across the sand to capture prey.<ref name=":6" /> When inland, it can also prey on [[insect]]s like beetles and flies, and [[spider]]s.<ref name=":4" /> The Javan plover has been observed foraging in dry, cleared rice-fields before seeding or after harvest.<ref name=":6" />

=== Reproduction ===
Very little information is available regarding the breeding behavior of the Javan plover. Breeding is suspected to occur year-round, during both wet and dry seasons.<ref name=Taufiqurrahman2013/> The peak breeding season is believed to take place from May to August.<ref name=":6" /> A second breeding season may also occur between September and January to February.<ref name=":6" /> In West Java, chicks have been observed as early as late April.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> In eastern Java, egg-laying occurs in June, September, and January, and hatching has been recorded in June and August, while courtship or territorial displays have been observed from May to August.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> Observations of breeding activity have been reported on Sulawesi in early October, in southeastern Sumatra and Timor-Leste in July, and on Bali in June.<ref name=Wiersma2023/>
[[File:Snail Pirenella cingulata (Gmelin, 1791) (52075477430).jpg|thumb|Shells of mud snails (''Pirenella cingulata'')]]
The Javan plover breeds in coastal and inland habitats far from human activity (2-5 km away), including open [[Dune slack|dune slacks]], shores of rivers and lakes, marshes, dry plains, rice fields, and dry aquaculture ponds.<ref name=Febrianto2016/><ref name=":6" /> The nest usually consists of a small cavity about 4 cm deep and is lined with shells of mud snails (''[[Pirenella cingulata]]'').<ref name=Febrianto2016/>
The nesting site is prepared collaboratively by both sexes.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> Ground-nesting shorebirds, including plovers, select their nesting site based on specific habitat characteristics, such as substrate particle size and vegetation cover.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dong-Yun |last2=Lee |first2=Ju-Hyun |last3=Son |first3=J.-J. |last4=Oh |first4=S.-J. |last5=Sung |first5=H.-C. |date=2025 |title=Estimating nesting habitat characteristics for the Kentish plover (''Anarhynchus alexandrinus'') with the effect of substrate and vegetation using a Bayesian network approach |journal=PLoS One |volume=20 |issue=6 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0325750 |article-number=e0325750 |pmid=40498874 |bibcode=2025PLoSO..2025750L |doi-access= free|pmc=12157252 }}</ref>

During the breeding season, some Javan plovers were observed attacking larger predatory birds to protect their chicks.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> Documented predators of both chicks and eggs include the [[long-tailed shrike]] (''Lanius schach''), the [[Sunda crow]] (''Corvus enca''), and the [[Javan mongoose]] (''Herpestes javanicus'').<ref name=Febrianto2016/>
[[File:Kentish Plover Nest (51114898251).jpg|thumb|Kentish plover (''Anarhynchus alexandrinus'') eggs, similar to Javan plover (''Anarhynchus javanicus'') eggs]]
Eggs of the Javan plover are light beige to dark olive-cream, and the shell has many black markings, typically interspersed with grayish or sepia undertones.<ref name=Wiersma2023/> A typical [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] consists of four eggs, each measuring approximately {{cvt|23|mm}} in width and 40 mm in length.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> Both parents contribute to incubation<ref name=Febrianto2016/><ref name=":6" /> They often dampen their underparts with water before taking turns sitting on the eggs, likely to keep the eggs from overheating during high temperatures, and the incubation lasts between 21 and 23 days.<ref name=Febrianto2016/>

Hatchlings closely resemble those of other small [[Charadriidae]] plovers.<ref name=":6" />
Chicks are [[Precociality and altriciality|precocious]] and leave the nest shortly after birth.<ref name=Febrianto2016/> After hatching, they can immediately walk and forage for food independently, although they remain under parental care for up to three months.<ref name=Febrianto2016/>

== Threats and conservation status ==
The Javan plover is listed as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn/> The global population is estimated between 2,000 and 6,000 individuals,<ref name=BirdLife2021/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Iqbal |first1=M. |last2=Taufiqurrahman |first2=I. |last3=Yordan |first3=K. |last4=Van Balen |first4=B. |date=2013 |title=The distribution, abundance and conservation status of the Javan Plover ''Charadrius javanicus'' |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/245030985 |journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin |volume=120 |issue=1 |pages=1–5}}</ref> Population numbers are thought to be stable.<ref name=iucn/> Current threats include egg-collecting, human disturbance, and coastal development under increasing tourism pressures in Southeast Asia.<ref name=iucn/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marasinghe |first1=S. |last2=Simpson |first2=G. D. |last3=Newsome |first3=D. |last4=Perera |first4=P. |date=2020 |title=Scoping Recreational Disturbance of Shorebirds to Inform the Agenda for Research and Management in Tropical Asia |journal=Tropical Life Sciences Research |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=51–78 |doi=10.21315/tlsr2020.31.2.4 |pmid=32922669 |pmc=7470479}}</ref>
